target.title: fix(exec): clean approval-pending chat prompts
target.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes the Telegram/Zollie approval loop symptom from the live test instance by separating two things that were being collapsed into one confusing chat prompt:

- approval-pending gateway/node execs, where no command is running yet and the result should just ask for approval
- real foreground fallback execs, where background execution is unavailable and the command actually does run synchronously

It also adds doctor preview/fix coverage for the older restricted-profile tool config shape that allowed `exec`/`write` but omitted their companion `process`/`edit` groups.

## RCA

The live logs did not point at Telegram delivery loss. Telegram sends were making it through. The bad behavior was command approval churn: the gateway had pending exec approvals waiting for user action, including one `exec.approval.waitDecision` span that sat for roughly 1,799,938 ms before the follow-up path handled it.

The screenshot made the confusing part visible. The bot first said the check was pending approval, then rendered a second card that said `Warning: background execution is disabled; running synchronously.` That warning was wrong for this approval state. For approval-gated gateway/node execs, the command has not started yet. The tool registers an approval request and returns the pending card immediately; if approved, the command continues later through the approval follow-up path.

The bug was in `createExecTool`. It added the background-disabled warning to the shared warning list before gateway approval routing. The approval renderer reuses that warning list, so the pending approval prompt inherited a warning that only makes sense after OpenClaw has decided to run the command in the foreground. That made Telegram look like it was running lost synchronous exec loops when it was really waiting on approval.

The second contributing issue was config drift. The instance had a restricted `messaging` tools profile with explicit `alsoAllow` entries for `exec` and `write`, plus configured `tools.exec`/`tools.fs`, but it did not also allow the companion capabilities `process` and `edit`. Current policy intentionally no longer widens those implicitly. That is good security behavior, but older configs can end up half-migrated: the user is allowed to start an exec/write path but not the companion management/edit tool that makes the experience coherent. The logs were warning about exactly that.

## What Changed

The exec tool now keeps the background-disabled warning staged until after gateway approval routing. If the command returns an approval-pending result, the warning is not shown and is not included in the approval request warning text. If OpenClaw really does run a foreground command because background execution is disabled, the existing warning is still shown.

Doctor now detects and repairs the older partial restricted-profile shape. It only repairs explicit partial grants: if a restricted `messaging` or `minimal` profile already explicitly allows `exec` while `tools.exec` is configured, doctor adds `process`; if it already explicitly allows `write` while `tools.fs` is configured, doctor adds `edit`. It does not touch complete grouped configs such as `group:runtime`/`group:fs`, coding/full profiles, or implicit-only configs.

I also documented the approval-gated exec exception in the exec docs and updated the gateway tool config docs to steer operators toward grouped capabilities and doctor repair.

## Why I’m Confident

The tests lock both sides of the behavior. There is a regression test proving approval-pending gateway exec does not include the misleading background-disabled warning, and a foreground failure test proving the real synchronous fallback warning still appears when it should. The doctor helper tests cover preview warnings, global repair, inherited agent repair, and no-op cases for complete groups, implicit configs, and coding profiles.

The fresh Testbox run synced only this branch’s changed files and passed the focused tests plus the changed gate on the expected `core`, `coreTests`, and `docs` lanes.

## Verification

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json` showed `core`, `coreTests`, and `docs`
- Testbox `tbx_01kqxehk7wevjj9memh7kdhy75`: `pnpm test:serial src/agents/bash-tools.exec.approval-id.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/tool-companion-allowlist-repair.test.ts` passed, 35 tests
- Testbox `tbx_01kqxehk7wevjj9memh7kdhy75`: `pnpm check:changed` passed on `core`, `coreTests`, and `docs`

Docs:

- https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/exec
- https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/config-tools

target.title: Experiment with agent worker runtime isolation
target.body:
## Summary

- add an experimental Node worker runtime path for Pi agent attempts, enabled through `agents.defaults.experimental.runtimeIsolation.mode: "worker"` or `OPENCLAW_AGENT_RUNTIME_WORKER=1`
- move attempt execution into a worker entrypoint with filesystem permission plumbing and build output support
- document the experimental setting and keep transcript persistence working by initializing context engines before parent-side CLI compaction
- serialize session-store writes with a `sessions.json.lock` file lock so concurrent worker/process isolates do not lose updates
- restore plugin runtime state inside worker attempts and resync parent-visible session-store state after worker attempts

## Why

The branch explores running each agent attempt in a separate worker so multiple agents can run in parallel while gaining a cleaner boundary for per-agent filesystem isolation. The first version keeps the main runtime contract intact and gates the behavior behind an explicit experimental switch.

The branch also fixes issues found during verification and review:

- the worker completed successfully, but parent-side transcript persistence resolved the default `legacy` context engine before the parent process had registered built-in engines
- session-store writes were only serialized inside one JS isolate, so concurrent workers/processes could overwrite each other when mutating the same `sessions.json`
- worker attempts started with fresh plugin globals, so plugin hooks and command prompt guidance were absent
- worker-side session-store mutations could leave the parent with stale in-memory session state

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Experimental worker runtime can run real local OpenClaw agent attempts successfully, parent-side transcript persistence no longer reports `Context engine "legacy" is not registered`, concurrent session-store updates preserve all writers, and the latest worker boundary fixes preserve plugin runtime restore plus parent session-store sync.

**Real environment tested:** Blacksmith Testbox via Crabbox, OpenAI auth from `openclaw-testbox-env`, provider/model `openai/gpt-5.4`, three worker-enabled agents (`alpha`, `beta`, `gamma`) launched in parallel.

**Exact proof:** Crabbox run https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/25417924262 hydrated Testbox `tbx_01kqxvq72qnz2v9av2gp5f7kft`, built the branch, then launched all three agents concurrently with `OPENCLAW_AGENT_RUNTIME_WORKER=1 node openclaw.mjs agent --local ... --json`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Built the PR on Testbox, then launched three local agent commands in parallel with worker isolation enabled: `OPENCLAW_AGENT_RUNTIME_WORKER=1 node openclaw.mjs agent --local --agent alpha --provider openai --model gpt-5.4 --json`, repeated for `beta` and `gamma`, followed by a `sessions.json` verification script that checked all three persisted session entries.

**Evidence after fix:** The remote verifier printed:

```text
MULTI_AGENT_WORKER_LIVE_OK
MULTI_AGENT_SESSION_STORE_OK
```

**Observed result after fix:** All three live OpenAI agent runs returned the exact expected payloads (`ALPHA_WORKER_OK`, `BETA_WORKER_OK`, `GAMMA_WORKER_OK`), each reported the embedded runner, and each persisted its session entry under the matching agent state directory. The latest local worker-thread regression run also passed `src/agents/worker-runtime/agent-runtime.test.ts` and `src/agents/worker-runtime/plugin-runtime.test.ts`, covering plugin runtime restore and parent session-store resync on worker success/error.

**What was not tested:** No GUI, mobile, or non-agent channel surfaces. For the latest review follow-up, a fresh `pnpm check:changed` Testbox run was attempted but the runner stayed queued, so it was stopped without result.

**Remaining note:** The parallel live run emitted two non-fatal `[tasks/registry] Failed to restore task registry` warnings, but the agent runs and session persistence completed. That looks like a separate task-registry startup/concurrency wart, not the worker runtime handoff fixed here.

## Verification

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `pnpm test src/config/sessions/store-writer.test.ts src/agents/worker-runtime/agent-runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/worker-runtime/agent-runtime.test.ts src/agents/worker-runtime/plugin-runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/worker-runtime/agent-runtime.ts src/agents/worker-runtime/agent-runtime.worker.ts src/agents/worker-runtime/agent-runtime.test.ts src/agents/worker-runtime/plugin-runtime.ts src/agents/worker-runtime/plugin-runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/config/sessions/store-writer.ts src/config/sessions/store-writer.test.ts docs/reference/session-management-compaction.md docs/concepts/experimental-features.md`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/config/sessions/store-writer.ts src/config/sessions/store-writer.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- Crabbox/Testbox: `pnpm test src/config/sessions/store-writer.test.ts src/agents/worker-runtime/agent-runtime.test.ts` passed in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/25417769087 before the live agent phase
- Crabbox/Testbox live multi-agent proof passed in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/25417924262 with `MULTI_AGENT_WORKER_LIVE_OK` and `MULTI_AGENT_SESSION_STORE_OK`
- post-commit `git diff --check`

target.title: fix(memory-core): exclude session-corpus files from short-term promotion (#77831)
target.body:
## Summary
- Dreaming writes session transcripts to `memory/.dreams/session-corpus/` inside the memory tree. The short-term promotion system tracked these paths in the recall store correctly (so dreaming phases can measure organic recall frequency), but also allowed them to pass through both promotion gates — causing 83% of recall-store entries to become session-corpus noise that polluted `MEMORY.md` with raw JSON, REM phase markers, and evidence lines.
- Added `isSessionCorpusPath()` helper that matches `memory/.dreams/session-corpus/*.txt|md` paths.
- Applied the guard in two places: (1) `recordShortTermRecalls` — corpus snippets are now skipped before entering the recall store as promotion candidates; (2) `rankShortTermPromotionCandidates` — existing corpus entries are skipped during candidate ranking.
- `isShortTermMemoryPath()` is unchanged so dreaming-phase signal tracking continues to work correctly.

Closes #77831

## Testing
```
pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts
```
```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  47 passed (47)
   Start at  19:36:25
   Duration  1.56s
```

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior: `rankShortTermPromotionCandidates` returned `memory/.dreams/session-corpus/YYYY-MM-DD.txt` entries as promotion candidates despite those files being internal corpus data; the new `isSessionCorpusPath()` guard excludes them while keeping daily memory notes eligible.
- Tested via targeted vitest test added in this PR. No live OpenClaw runtime available — please apply maintainer `proof:` override or advise on evidence format.

target.title: fix(openai-compat): derive stable session from X-Chat-Id / X-Thread-Id headers (#78091)
target.body:
## Summary
- Each request to the OpenAI-compat `/v1/chat/completions` endpoint generated a random-UUID session key when the `user` field was absent, causing Open-WebUI (and similar frontends) to create a new isolated session per message instead of reusing one session per conversation.
- Now checks `X-Chat-Id` (Open-WebUI) and `X-Thread-Id` (Langchain / other clients) headers first; when either is present a deterministic session key `<prefix>-chat:<id>` is derived, so all turns in one conversation land in the same session.
- `X-OpenClaw-Session-Key` and the `user`-field path are fully preserved and still take higher precedence.

Closes #78091

## Testing
```
pnpm vitest run src/gateway/http-utils.request-context.test.ts
```
```
 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  51 passed (51)
   Start at  19:24:25
   Duration  3.97s
```

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior: Open-WebUI sends `X-Chat-Id: <uuid>` per the [Open-WebUI OpenAI backend docs](https://docs.openwebui.com/getting-started/advanced-topics/api-endpoints) when configured to use a custom OpenAI-compatible backend; without this fix every message creates `agent:<id>:openai:<random-uuid>`, reproducing 44+ dead sessions per conversation as shown in the issue.
- Tested via targeted vitest test added in this PR. No live OpenClaw runtime available — please apply maintainer `proof:` override or advise on evidence format.

target.title: Signal: match allowlist on either uuid or e164 alias
target.body:
## Summary

`channels.signal.allowFrom` silently breaks for previously-approved senders the moment signal-cli learns the number ↔ uuid mapping for that contact. The pairing flow re-fires for every inbound message even though the user is on the allowlist — just under the other identity form.

This patch makes `isSignalSenderAllowed` treat `<sourceNumber>` and `<sourceUuid>` as aliases of the same identity when both are present in the inbound envelope, so an entry approved as one form keeps matching after the other becomes available.

### Reproduction

1. Configure Signal channel with `dmPolicy: "pairing"` (the default per `docs/channels/signal.md`). Account is fresh — signal-cli has only the user's UUID, not their E.164.
2. User DMs the bot. They get a pairing code. Operator runs `openclaw pairing approve signal <code>`. The approval lands in `<credentialsDir>/signal-default-allowFrom.json` as `uuid:<id>`. Replies work.
3. Later, configure a cron / scheduled task that sends to the same user **by E.164**. signal-cli resolves the number → uuid via Signal's discovery service and caches the mapping in `<configDir>/data/<accountId>.d/`.
4. The user replies again. signal-cli's SSE event now carries **both** `sourceNumber` and `sourceUuid`. `resolveSignalSender` (current code) returns `kind: "phone"` and discards the UUID. `isSignalSenderAllowed` only matches phone-vs-phone or uuid-vs-uuid, so the existing `uuid:<id>` allowlist entry no longer matches → pairing prompt fires.

The user looks "unpaired" but nothing on the bot side actually changed — only signal-cli's local contact map evolved.

### Fix

`extensions/signal/src/identity.ts`:

- `SignalSender` gains an optional `aliases?: { e164?: string; uuid?: string }` field. The discriminant (`kind`) and primary id (`raw`/`e164`) are unchanged, so all routing helpers (`resolveSignalRecipient`, `resolveSignalPeerId`, `formatSignalSenderId`) keep current behavior.
- `resolveSignalSender` populates `aliases.uuid` when both `sourceNumber` and `sourceUuid` are present on the envelope.
- `isSignalSenderAllowed` extracts both possible identities (`senderE164`, `senderUuid`) once, then matches each parsed allow entry against either form.

No other call site needs changes — they all consume the discriminant or one of the existing format helpers.

### Why I think this is the right shape

- Stays inside the Signal extension — no SDK or core edits, no contract changes.
- Backwards-compatible at the type level: `aliases` is optional. Existing call sites that construct `SignalSender` literally (only `core.test.ts` does this in this repo) keep compiling.
- Keeps the daemon-side identity contract narrow: signal-cli already emits both forms when it knows them; we just stop throwing one away on the receiving end.
- Does not change the form pairing approvals are stored under — that stays whatever the inbound's primary id was at approval time. The patch only fixes lookup.

### Out of scope (followups worth considering)

- Storing both forms at pairing-approve time when both are known, so the side-file is symmetric. That's a `pairing/pairing-store.ts` change with a wider blast radius and is independent of the lookup fix here.
- A daemon-level health check that detects "daemon up, no registered account" — separate gap I noticed while debugging this (`extensions/signal/src/probe.ts:24` and `sse-reconnect.ts:48` will both report healthy against an account-less signal-cli). Worth its own PR.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test extensions/signal/src/core.test.ts` — 38 passed (7 new, including the regression scenario).
- [x] `pnpm test extensions/signal` — 214 passed across 22 files.
- [x] `pnpm tsgo` (core), `pnpm tsgo:extensions`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` — all clean.
- [x] `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` on the touched files — clean.
- [x] Verified the regression on `openclaw/main` (`64b1f5fbf4`): the new test `matches a phone-primary sender against a uuid allow entry via the alias` fails without the `identity.ts` change and passes with it.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
target.author: fransqaas

target.title: fix(sessions): unbind conversation bindings when missing transcripts are pruned
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: When \`sessions cleanup --fix-missing\` removes a session store entry because its transcript file is missing, the matching conversation binding is left intact. Subsequent messages resolve the stale binding, route to the pruned session key, and the gateway silently spawns a fresh in-memory session per message that is never persisted. Reported in #77871.
- **Root Cause**: \`pruneMissingTranscriptEntries\` in \`src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.ts\` collects session keys to remove, then \`applySessionEntryLifecycleMutation\` commits the removal — but neither step calls \`getSessionBindingService().unbind()\` for the affected keys. The reset and delete paths (\`emitSessionUnboundLifecycleEvent\` in \`session-reset-service.ts\`) enforce this unbind contract; the cleanup path omitted it entirely. \`resolveRuntimeConversationBindingRoute\` in \`src/channels/plugins/binding-routing.ts\` also carried a dead optional \`targetSessionExists\` predicate never passed by any caller and superseded by the \`isCronRunSessionKey\` guard already present on main.
- **Fix**: After \`applySessionEntryLifecycleMutation\` returns \`lifecycleResult.removedSessionKeys\`, filter \`missingRemovals\` by that set and call \`getSessionBindingService().unbind({ targetSessionKey, reason: "cleanup-missing-transcript" })\` for each confirmed removal. This mirrors the contract already enforced by session reset and delete. Remove the dead \`targetSessionExists\` parameter from \`resolveRuntimeConversationBindingRoute\`.
- **What changed**:
  - \`src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.ts\` — import \`getSessionBindingService\`; after lifecycle mutation, unbind each missing-transcript key confirmed in \`removedSessionKeys\`
  - \`src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.test.ts\` — new file; 5 cases covering single-key unbind, multi-key unbind, dry-run no-op, \`fixMissing: false\` no-op, and existing-transcript no-op
  - \`src/channels/plugins/binding-routing.ts\` — remove dead \`targetSessionExists\` optional parameter and its if-block
  - \`src/channels/plugins/binding-routing.test.ts\` — remove 4 \`targetSessionExists\` test cases; remove unused import
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: The preview/dry-run pass in \`runSessionsCleanup\` is unchanged. \`session-reset-service.ts\`, \`session-binding-service.ts\`, \`current-conversation-bindings.ts\` unchanged. No changes to any extension code. Config surface unchanged. Plugin surface unchanged. No dependency changes.

### Reproduction

1. Start the gateway with a channel conversation bound to a persistent session (\`agent:review:acp:session-1\`).
2. Stop the gateway; delete the session transcript file from the state directory while leaving the session store entry and binding intact.
3. Run \`openclaw sessions cleanup --fix-missing\`.
4. Send a message on the bound channel.
5. **Before this PR**: Cleanup removes the store entry but leaves the binding; step 4 routes to the phantom key, spawns an ephemeral in-memory session, and persists nothing.
6. **After this PR**: Step 3 also unbinds the stale binding; step 4 falls through to a fresh persisted session.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed (#77871)**: Missing-transcript prune in \`runSessionsCleanup\` left conversation bindings pointing to pruned session keys, silently spawning ephemeral sessions on every subsequent message. The fix unbinds each key confirmed removed by \`applySessionEntryLifecycleMutation\`; only keys present in \`lifecycleResult.removedSessionKeys\` are unbound, so entries whose transcripts still exist on disk are not touched.

**Real environment tested (Linux, Node 22 — Vitest runtime-config and channels suites)**:  \`pnpm test src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.test.ts src/channels/plugins/binding-routing.test.ts --reporter=verbose\`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: \`pnpm test src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.test.ts src/channels/plugins/binding-routing.test.ts --reporter=verbose\`

**Evidence after fix (verbatim output)**:
```text
✓ |runtime-config| src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.test.ts > runSessionsCleanup: conversation binding unbind after missing-transcript prune > unbinds conversation bindings for session entries whose transcripts are pruned 14ms
✓ |runtime-config| src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.test.ts > runSessionsCleanup: conversation binding unbind after missing-transcript prune > unbinds each pruned key when multiple session transcripts are missing 2ms
✓ |runtime-config| src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.test.ts > runSessionsCleanup: conversation binding unbind after missing-transcript prune > does not unbind or touch the store during dry-run 1ms
✓ |runtime-config| src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.test.ts > runSessionsCleanup: conversation binding unbind after missing-transcript prune > does not unbind when fixMissing is not requested 1ms
✓ |runtime-config| src/config/sessions/cleanup-service.test.ts > runSessionsCleanup: conversation binding unbind after missing-transcript prune > does not unbind entries whose transcripts still exist on disk 2ms
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  5 passed (5)

✓ |channels| src/channels/plugins/binding-routing.test.ts > runtime conversation binding route > rewrites the route to a runtime-bound ACP session and touches the binding 117ms
✓ |channels| src/channels/plugins/binding-routing.test.ts > runtime conversation binding route > touches plugin-owned bindings without rewriting the channel route 3ms
✓ |channels| src/channels/plugins/binding-routing.test.ts > runtime conversation binding route > ignores runtime bindings that target isolated cron run sessions 2ms
✓ |channels| src/channels/plugins/binding-routing.test.ts > ensureConfiguredBindingRouteReady > returns a bounded failure when target readiness never settles 5ms
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  4 passed (4)
```

**Observed result after fix**: All 5 new cleanup-service cases pass; unbind is called exactly once per confirmed-removed key, not called during dry-run, not called when \`fixMissing\` is false, not called when the transcript file exists on disk. All 4 existing binding-routing cases pass with the dead \`targetSessionExists\` code removed.

**What was not tested**: Live gateway round-trip with a real channel session and real on-disk transcript deletion. The test suite mocks \`applySessionEntryLifecycleMutation\` to return a controlled \`removedSessionKeys\` set; the production function's own removal logic is covered by its separate tests in \`session-accessor\`.

**Repro confirmation**: Each of the 5 new \`cleanup-service.test.ts\` cases fails on \`main\` (no \`unbind\` call in the production path) and passes after this patch.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: \`unbind\` is an additional async call per pruned entry in the apply path. **Mitigation**: The loop is bounded by the number of confirmed-removed entries; \`--fix-missing\` is an operator-triggered maintenance pass, not a hot path. \`unbind\` is the same service call already used by the reset and delete paths.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway
- [x] Sessions / storage
- [x] Channel binding routing

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #77871

target.title: fix: show in-progress status for channel runs
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users sending messages through a channel such as Weixin would not see the Control UI run status switch to "In progress" while the agent loop was running.

WebChat-started runs already showed the active state, but channel-started reply runs could leave the session row looking idle in Control UI.

## Why This Change Was Made

This routes every gateway session payload that projects `hasActiveRun` through a single visible active-run helper. The helper keeps the existing Control UI chat abort-controller behavior and also checks the embedded/channel reply-run registry by `sessionId`, so list, startup/history, session-change, and transcript-message snapshots all agree.

## User Impact

Users can now watch channel-triggered agent work in Control UI and see the same "In progress" status they already get for WebChat-triggered runs.

## Evidence

  - `pnpm test src/gateway/server-session-events.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.abort-agent-scope.test.ts`

target.title: fix: keep Android gateway settings save idempotent
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where Android users could tap Gateway Settings `Save & Connect` on an already-working app and unexpectedly re-enter gateway pairing or node capability approval when no meaningful setting changed.

The fragile behavior made Gateway Settings feel destructive: unchanged fields could still clear setup/device auth, endpoint edits could accidentally carry stale credentials, and the device-pairing admin message blurred phone pairing with node capability approval.

## Why This Change Was Made

Gateway Settings now uses the shared gateway connection resolver instead of rebuilding credential fallback inline, and it preserves saved setup/shared auth only for the unchanged saved manual endpoint. Endpoint changes drop saved credentials unless the user supplies replacement auth, and setup-code replacement now requires an explicit confirmation before clearing setup credentials and device tokens.

The copy around device-pairing admin access was also updated to explain that approving new phone pairing requires a gateway admin session, while node capability approval is a separate `nodes approve <request id>` flow.

## User Impact

Users can safely use `Save & Connect` from Android Gateway Settings without treating the screen like a destructive re-pairing action. A no-op save or reconnect path keeps existing gateway/device auth intact, while risky setup-code replacement is called out before it can trigger reapproval.

Operators get clearer wording when the current gateway sign-in can list nodes but cannot approve new phone pairing.

## Evidence

- Focused Android unit tests:
  - `JAVA_HOME='/Applications/Android Studio.app/Contents/jbr/Contents/Home' ANDROID_HOME="$HOME/Library/Android/sdk" ANDROID_SDK_ROOT="$HOME/Library/Android/sdk" ./gradlew --no-daemon :app:testPlayDebugUnitTest --tests ai.openclaw.app.ui.GatewayConfigResolverTest --tests ai.openclaw.app.GatewayBootstrapAuthTest --tests ai.openclaw.app.ui.SettingsScreensTest`
  - Result: `BUILD SUCCESSFUL`

commands.author: Takhoffman

target.title: fix: show in-progress status for channel runs
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users sending messages through a channel such as Weixin would not see the Control UI run status switch to "In progress" while the agent loop was running.

WebChat-started runs already showed the active state, but channel-started reply runs could leave the session row looking idle in Control UI.

## Why This Change Was Made

This routes every gateway session payload that projects `hasActiveRun` through a single visible active-run helper. The helper keeps the existing Control UI chat abort-controller behavior and also checks the embedded/channel reply-run registry by `sessionId`, so list, startup/history, session-change, and transcript-message snapshots all agree.

## User Impact

Users can now watch channel-triggered agent work in Control UI and see the same "In progress" status they already get for WebChat-triggered runs.

## Evidence

- Focused tests:
  - `pnpm test src/gateway/server-session-events.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.abort-agent-scope.test.ts`
  - Result: 4 files passed, 50 tests passed.
- Formatting/diff guard:
  - `git diff --check HEAD`
  - Result: passed.
- Local ClawSweeper review:
  - `pnpm run review -- --local-only --local-range --target-repo openclaw/openclaw --target-dir ../openclaw --base origin/main --codex-timeout-ms 600000`
  - Result: `review_status: complete`, `Overall correctness: patch is correct`, `Full review comments: none`.
- Manual video proof:
  - Before fix: 
  

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/bac7f0d8-c752-46d8-bf18-b41e6bd8cbbd


  - After fix: 


https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f399bae2-fe16-47e5-90c4-92e5170d5934

target.title: fix(openai): enable prompt cache keys for Azure
target.body:
## Summary
- centralize Azure OpenAI chat-completions prompt-cache-key detection in the shared completions compat defaults
- apply that default in the legacy provider builder, boundary-aware OpenAI transport, and embedded-agent extra-params retention eligibility path
- include traditional Azure hosts, Azure AI Foundry project hosts, and regional `.api.cognitive.microsoft.com` Foundry hosts
- preserve Azure long prompt-cache retention (`prompt_cache_retention: "24h"`) when callers request `cacheRetention: "long"`, and preserve explicit `cacheRetention: "none"`
- add regression coverage for Azure prompt-cache key emission, long/disabled retention, extra-params propagation, and both request paths

## What Problem This Solves
OpenClaw already gates OpenAI-compatible prompt cache keys behind `compat.supportsPromptCacheKey`, but Azure OpenAI chat-completions endpoints were not auto-detected. Azure users therefore needed explicit per-model compat config before stable session prompt-cache keys were forwarded, which can silently reduce prompt-cache hit opportunities for long stable prefixes.

The change treats Azure OpenAI chat-completions endpoints (`azure-openai`, `.openai.azure.com`, `.cognitiveservices.azure.com`, `.services.ai.azure.com`, and `.api.cognitive.microsoft.com`) as prompt-cache-key capable by default. The default now lives in the shared completions compat path used by both request builders and by embedded-agent extra-param cache-retention eligibility; explicit `compat.supportsPromptCacheKey` / `compat.supportsLongCacheRetention` overrides continue to win.

## Evidence
- Searched existing issues/PRs before filing. Found related prompt-cache work (#78747, #82939, #94518, #83709, #69272, #67427), but no open PR specifically enabling Azure OpenAI chat-completions prompt cache keys by default.
- Verified upstream `main` only enabled chat-completions `prompt_cache_key` for `api.openai.com` or explicit `compat.supportsPromptCacheKey`; Azure endpoint detection was absent in both relevant request paths and extra-params retention eligibility.
- Added regression tests that confirm Azure OpenAI chat completions forward `prompt_cache_key`, preserve `prompt_cache_retention: "24h"` for long retention, preserve `cacheRetention: "none"`, cover the regional Azure Foundry host, and propagate embedded-agent `cacheRetention` for detected Azure models.
- Live Azure proof, redacted terminal output from `thomais2026v2-resource.openai.azure.com/openai/v1/chat/completions` using `gpt-5.5`, `prompt_cache_key`, and `prompt_cache_retention: "24h"`:

```json
{"label":"warmup","status":200,"ok":true,"model":"gpt-5.5-2026-04-24","elapsedMs":1328,"promptTokens":4090,"completionTokens":8,"cachedTokens":0,"hasPromptCacheKey":true,"requestedRetention":"24h","errorCode":null,"errorMessage":null}
{"label":"repeat-prefix","status":200,"ok":true,"model":"gpt-5.5-2026-04-24","elapsedMs":1204,"promptTokens":4090,"completionTokens":5,"cachedTokens":3840,"hasPromptCacheKey":true,"requestedRetention":"24h","errorCode":null,"errorMessage":null}
```

## Test plan
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/llm/providers/openai-completions.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit.config.ts`
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts`
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/openai-completions-compat.test.ts`
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner-extraparams.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/extra-params.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner-extraparams.test.ts src/agents/openai-completions-compat.ts src/agents/openai-completions-compat.test.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts src/llm/providers/openai-completions.ts src/llm/providers/openai-completions.test.ts`

commands.author: christopheraaronhogg

target.title: improve: make native chat scrolling reader-managed
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Native chat views currently move the transcript as messages, tool rows, and streaming text arrive. That can pull readers away from content they are reviewing in long or actively streaming conversations.

Fixes #98255.

## Why This Change Was Made

This brings iOS and Android chat scrolling closer to reader-managed streaming behavior:

- restore a saved/opened conversation near the latest user turn instead of the absolute bottom
- follow streaming content only while the reader is still following the live edge/current follow target
- let assistant/tool/stream content arrive offscreen after the reader scrolls away
- show an explicit Jump to latest control that returns to the newest reply and resumes following
- split Android initial restore targets from live-follow targets so active streaming can keep the prompt anchor without forcing every reopen to the bottom

## User Impact

- Reopening a session lands near the most recent user prompt for context.
- Sending a new message intentionally moves that new turn into view.
- Scrolling away stops automatic movement while new content continues arriving.
- A visible Jump to latest action appears when newer content arrives out of view.

## Native Behavior Proof

Proof artifacts are pinned here:

https://github.com/christopheraaronhogg/openclaw/tree/35232cbc99fe448ffba2431d8304976389430950/docs/proof/native-chat-scroll/2026-06-30

- iOS simulator recording: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/christopheraaronhogg/openclaw/35232cbc99fe448ffba2431d8304976389430950/docs/proof/native-chat-scroll/2026-06-30/ios-chat-scroll-proof.mp4
- Android emulator recording: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/christopheraaronhogg/openclaw/35232cbc99fe448ffba2431d8304976389430950/docs/proof/native-chat-scroll/2026-06-30/android-chat-scroll-proof.mp4
- iOS Jump-visible still: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/christopheraaronhogg/openclaw/35232cbc99fe448ffba2431d8304976389430950/docs/proof/native-chat-scroll/2026-06-30/ios-jump-visible.png
- iOS after-Jump still: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/christopheraaronhogg/openclaw/35232cbc99fe448ffba2431d8304976389430950/docs/proof/native-chat-scroll/2026-06-30/ios-after-jump.png
- Android Jump-visible still: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/christopheraaronhogg/openclaw/35232cbc99fe448ffba2431d8304976389430950/docs/proof/native-chat-scroll/2026-06-30/android-jump-visible.png
- Android after-Jump still: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/christopheraaronhogg/openclaw/35232cbc99fe448ffba2431d8304976389430950/docs/proof/native-chat-scroll/2026-06-30/android-after-jump.png

Proof setup:

- iOS uses the SwiftUI preview host for the actual shared `OpenClawChatUI` chat view and view model.
- Android uses a debug-only `chat-proof` screenshot scene mounted on the actual `ChatMessageListCard`.
- Both proof lanes use delayed stream/tool rows. Runtime snapshots/dumps confirmed Jump is visible while the visible transcript remains on restored context, and after Jump the iOS visible text includes the tool/live rows with Jump gone.

## Evidence

- PASS `swift build --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --target OpenClawChatUI`
- PASS `ANDROID_HOME=$HOME/Library/Android/sdk ANDROID_SDK_ROOT=$HOME/Library/Android/sdk ./gradlew :app:testPlayDebugUnitTest --tests ai.openclaw.app.ui.chat.ChatTimelineTest`
- PASS `ANDROID_HOME=$HOME/Library/Android/sdk ANDROID_SDK_ROOT=$HOME/Library/Android/sdk ./gradlew :app:installPlayDebug`
- PASS `git diff --check`
- INFO `ANDROID_HOME=$HOME/Library/Android/sdk ANDROID_SDK_ROOT=$HOME/Library/Android/sdk ./gradlew :app:ktlintCheck` still fails on existing untouched files: `apps/android/app/src/main/java/ai/openclaw/app/GatewayExecApprovals.kt:100` and `apps/android/app/src/main/java/ai/openclaw/app/ui/design/ClawSurfaces.kt:3`

commands.author: Solvely-Colin

target.title: feat: add one-step gateway setup pairing
target.body:
Related: #98242
Supersedes the gateway half of #98243.

## What Problem This Solves

Users setting up OpenClaw Mobile had to scan or paste a long setup code and then complete separate device/node approval steps. That made QR/setup-code onboarding feel like many steps instead of the intended one-step mobile setup.

## Why This Change Was Made

This makes Setup Code and QR onboarding the primary one-step path: the app receives the Gateway URL/bootstrap auth from the setup-code payload, connects, and the Gateway silently approves only fresh baseline mobile device/node surfaces. The setup short code is a gateway-local convenience for typing the same setup-code payload on reachable local/co-resident Gateway flows; the long setup-code QR remains the durable fallback.

The node approval boundary is platform-specific for current iOS and Android app-declared capabilities/commands/permissions. Cross-platform, plugin, arbitrary, upgraded, or dangerous node command surfaces remain pending unless the Gateway owner explicitly allows them.

## User Impact

A fresh mobile app can scan the QR or enter the setup code, save the Gateway URL/bootstrap auth, connect, receive bounded node/operator handoff tokens, and land connected without a separate manual approval step. Operators still get explicit pending approval flows for non-baseline or upgraded node surfaces.

## Evidence

target.title: fix(feishu): carry forward DM fallback and topic labels
target.body:
## Summary

Carry forward the remaining Feishu display-name behavior from #38958 after #51032 already landed the group-name session label fix.

This replacement should stay narrow:
- keep #51032's group-name implementation as the baseline
- add/repair DM display-name fallback through chatMembers with contact fallback
- preserve resolveSenderNames opt-out semantics
- scope sender/direct-member caches by account and relevant chat/sender identifiers
- surface topic/root labels only for topic-scoped Feishu sessions
- propagate permission guidance when DM fallback lookup hits a Feishu permission error

Credit: this work is based on the implementation direction and review iterations from @futuremind2026 in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/38958. The already-merged group-name baseline came from @jnuyao in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/51032.

## Validation

- pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot-sender-name.test.ts
- pnpm exec vitest run src/config/sessions.test.ts
- pnpm check:changed

ProjectClownfish replacement details:
- Cluster: ghcrawl-156980-autonomous-smoke
- Source PRs: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/38958
- Credit: Credit @futuremind2026 and source PR https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/38958 for the broader Feishu DM fallback, cache-scoping, permission guidance, and topic-label implementation direction.; Preserve @jnuyao and merged PR https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/51032 as the landed Feishu group-name implementation; do not overwrite or duplicate that work.; Mention in the PR body that this replacement carries forward the remaining behavior from #38958 because ProjectClownfish cannot safely update the original contributor branch.
- Validation: pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot-sender-name.test.ts; pnpm exec vitest run src/config/sessions.test.ts; pnpm check:changed

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: test(plugins): cover filtered bundle MCP servers
target.body:
## Summary

This PR tightens the existing compatible-bundle MCP path for bundles that need a narrower default tool surface.

- Adds regression coverage that a Codex-compatible bundle `.mcp.json` preserves `toolFilter.include`, `toolFilter.exclude`, and a relative bundle data root.
- Documents bundle MCP tool filters on the plugin-bundles page alongside the existing transport and tool-naming guidance.
- Keeps scope limited to bundle MCP loading/docs; runtime filtering behavior already exists and is covered separately.

Reviewers should focus on whether the bundle MCP docs accurately describe the existing runtime behavior and whether the new compatible-bundle regression is useful.

## Linked context

No issue filed.

Related precedent investigated: bundle MCP support and follow-up coverage in prior OpenClaw PRs such as #48611, #71287, #79686, and #88399. This PR follows that existing bundle MCP seam.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: Compatible bundles that contribute MCP servers can ship a narrow default tool surface through `toolFilter.include` and `toolFilter.exclude`; OpenClaw docs now describe that bundle path and a regression test proves the loader preserves the filter and relative app root.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `test/filtered-bundle-mcp`, commit `d5bffb3dc0`, with an isolated temporary `HOME`, `OPENCLAW_HOME`, and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a temporary Codex-compatible bundle containing `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`, `.mcp.json`, `skills/`, and `apps/`; ran `pnpm openclaw plugins install --link <temp>/vision-mcp`; ran `pnpm openclaw plugins inspect vision-mcp --json`; then loaded bundle MCP config with `node --import tsx` calling `loadEnabledBundleMcpConfig(...)` against the isolated home/config.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the temporary real OpenClaw setup:

```text
Proof root: /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins install --link /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp
Linked plugin path: /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp
Restart the gateway to load plugins.
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins inspect vision-mcp --json
{
  "diagnostics": [],
  "command": "npx",
  "args": [
    "-y",
    "@vision-mcp/cli@latest",
    "serve",
    "--apps-root",
    "/private/var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp/apps"
  ],
  "cwdEndsWith": true,
  "toolFilter": {
    "include": [
      "capsule.*",
      "vision_map.snapshot",
      "vision_map.list_apps",
      "vision_map.list_workflows",
      "vision_map.describe_workflow",
      "vision_map.describe",
      "vision_map.detect_state",
      "vision_map.list_actions",
      "vision_map.describe_action",
      "vision_map.perform_action",
      "vision_map.run_workflow",
      "vision_map.verify",
      "vision_map.repair_minimal",
      "vision_map.export_trace"
    ],
    "exclude": [
      "vision_map.click_at",
      "vision_map.type_text",
      "vision_map.press_key",
      "vision_map.scroll",
      "vision_map.init",
      "vision_map.apply_patch",
      "vision_map.add_control",
      "vision_map.commit_*",
      "vision_map.harvest_session"
    ]
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: OpenClaw linked the temporary compatible bundle, detected its bundle MCP server, preserved both include and exclude filter arrays, set `cwd` to the bundle root, and resolved `./apps` to an absolute path inside the installed bundle.

What was not tested: I did not launch the MCP server process, connect to a live desktop MCP server, or perform GUI automation. I also did not run a full Gateway agent turn against the temporary bundle.

Proof limitations or environment constraints: The proof uses a temporary local compatible bundle and the real OpenClaw plugin installer/loader, but the MCP server command itself is not started because this PR changes documentation and loader regression coverage, not MCP runtime execution behavior.

## Tests and validation

Commands run:

```text
node scripts/test-projects.mjs -- src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts --reporter=verbose
pnpm format:check src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts
git diff --check
pnpm check:docs
codex review --base origin/main
```

Regression coverage added: `src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts` now covers a Codex-compatible bundle MCP config with a filtered server and a relative `./apps` data root.

Validation results:

```text
src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts: 1 file passed, 8 tests passed
pnpm format:check src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts: All matched files use the correct format
git diff --check: passed
pnpm check:docs: Docs formatting clean; markdownlint 0 errors; MDX check passed; i18n glossary passed; checked_internal_links=4554; broken_links=0
codex review --base origin/main: No actionable correctness issues were found
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No runtime behavior change; docs now describe existing bundle MCP filter behavior.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No runtime change. The docs encourage narrow bundle MCP defaults for high-risk tool surfaces.

What is the highest-risk area? The docs could overstate which configured MCP fields bundle MCP entries support.

How is that risk mitigated? Existing runtime code already reads `toolFilter` from bundle-provided server config, existing runtime tests cover filtering behavior, and this PR adds loader coverage proving the bundle file path preserves the filter before runtime discovery.

## Current review state

AI-assisted: yes.

Next action: maintainer review.

target.title: fix(mattermost): bound null-body error response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`readMattermostError()` had a `!res.body` fallback that bypassed the bounded reader and called native `res.json()` / `res.text()` directly. For a null-body error response with a JSON content-type (a 401/503 with `content-type: application/json` and an empty body — common from reverse proxies and gateways), `await res.json()` throws `SyntaxError: Unexpected end of JSON input`. That rejection propagates out of `readMattermostError()`, so the caller's intended `Mattermost API <status>: unknown error` is replaced by a confusing JSON-parse crash. It was also the last remaining unbounded `json()` / `text()` read in this error path after #96033 bounded the success path.

Affected surface: `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.ts` — `readMattermostError()`, reached by `client.request()`, the file-upload path, and `probe.ts`.

## Why This Change Was Made

The `!res.body` special-case is removed entirely, so every error read goes through the existing bounded `readResponseTextLimited` (8 KiB) and the JSON `message` is parsed from that bounded text. `readResponseTextLimited` returns `""` when there is no readable body, so a null-body response now yields an empty detail instead of throwing — and all three callers already coalesce that to a clean message (`detail || "unknown error"` / `detail || res.statusText`). One canonical read path, no second buffering strategy.

Net change is a deletion (`client.ts` +0/−10): removes the divergent branch rather than adding code.

## User Impact

Operators and agents talking to self-hosted or misbehaving Mattermost servers get a clean, accurate API error (`Mattermost API 503 Service Unavailable: unknown error`) on null-body JSON error responses, instead of an internal `Unexpected end of JSON input` crash — and every error read now goes through the bounded reader.

## Evidence

**1. Real-behavior proof with a negative control (real HTTP).** A harness drives the **real exported** `readMattermostError` and the **real** `createMattermostClient().request()` (through the real `fetchWithSsrFGuard` SSRF path, `allowPrivateNetwork: true` for localhost) against a real `node:http` server returning `503 Service Unavailable` with `content-type: application/json` and **no body**. The negative control runs the deleted branch's exact logic (`await res.json()`) on the same response.

```
$ tsx mm-proof.mts
Server returns 503 Service Unavailable, content-type application/json, NO body

[NEGATIVE CONTROL] old `!res.body` json branch: await res.json()
PASS: old branch crashes on null-body JSON error — THREW SyntaxError: Unexpected end of JSON input

[WITH FIX] readMattermostError(real null-body 503 json response)
PASS: returns empty string, no throw — detail=""

[WITH FIX] createMattermostClient().request() against null-body 503
PASS: clean bounded API error (no JSON crash) — "Mattermost API 503 Service Unavailable: unknown error"

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

Without the fix the null-body JSON error path throws `SyntaxError: Unexpected end of JSON input`; with the fix `readMattermostError` returns `""` and the client surfaces the clean `... 503 Service Unavailable: unknown error`.

**2. Committed regression tests** (`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.test.ts`) cover: null-body JSON errors never call `response.json()` / `response.text()`; bounded JSON `message` fields still parse from normal bodies; and a guarded null-body failure surfaced end-to-end through `createMattermostClient().request()`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  27 passed (27)
```

**3. Lint on the changed file:**

```
$ node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.ts   # exit 0
```

**What was NOT tested:** I did not run against a live production Mattermost server. The null-body JSON error condition is reproduced with a real local HTTP server over the real SSRF-guarded fetch path, including the negative control showing the pre-fix crash.

AI-assisted: implementation, tests, and the proof harness were drafted with agent assistance; all commands above were run locally.

target.title: docs(memory): align descriptors and docs with recursive memory/**/*.md
target.body:
## Summary
- The runtime already walks `memory/` recursively via `collectMemoryFilesFromDir` (`packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts`), so nested files like `memory/YYYY-MM/YYYY-MM-DD.md` are indexed and searched. But several agent-facing descriptors and public docs still said `memory/*.md`, which can mislead agents into thinking the index is flat.
- This PR updates the remaining stale references to `memory/**/*.md`. No runtime behavior change.

## Files touched
- `extensions/memory-core/index.ts` — lazy `memory_search` / `memory_get` descriptors
- `extensions/memory-core/src/tools.ts` — concrete `memory_search` / `memory_get` descriptions
- `extensions/memory-core/src/prompt-section.ts` — system-prompt recall guidance
- `src/config/schema.help.ts` — `agents.defaults.memorySearch` help text
- `docs/concepts/memory-builtin.md`, `memory.md`, `system-prompt.md`
- `docs/gateway/config-agents.md`, `docs/reference/token-use.md`
- `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256` regenerated via `pnpm config:docs:gen`

## Note
This supersedes #72748. That PR also carried QMD watcher changes that were independently resolved upstream by #81802 (`watch-settle.ts`), so the surviving value was just this wording alignment. Splitting it out as a small, behavior-neutral PR for easier review.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Agent-facing descriptors and public docs said `memory/*.md` even though the runtime already walks `memory/` recursively and indexes nested files such as `memory/YYYY-MM/YYYY-MM-DD.md`. This PR aligns the wording to `memory/**/*.md` so agents and readers see what is actually indexed. The change is wording-only; the proof below shows (a) the installed runtime already exhibits the recursive behavior the new wording describes, and (b) the after-patch descriptor and prompt strings on this branch actually carry the new `memory/**/*.md` wording when imported at runtime.

**Real environment tested**: Installed OpenClaw `2026.5.20` (npm global, current `latest` published build, ahead of the `acf265d` base this PR branches from), against the live workspace `~/.openclaw/workspace-moku` used by the gateway. Agent `main`, local embedding provider, per-agent SQLite memory store at `~/.openclaw/memory/main.sqlite`. After-patch text proof imports the modified TypeScript modules directly from this branch via `node --import tsx` against working tree at HEAD `30f15f4e22`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

Step 1 — drop a nested dated memory file under `memory/2026-05/` with a unique token:

```
cat > ~/.openclaw/workspace-moku/memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md <<'EOF'
Recursive memory search proof.
Unique token for PR 85899 smoke test: PROOFTOKEN_QXZ7K_NESTED_RECURSIVE_MEMORY
This file lives at memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md
EOF
```

Step 2 — reindex the live agent's memory store:

```
openclaw memory index
```

Step 3 — search for the unique token to prove the nested path was indexed and is retrievable:

```
openclaw memory search "PROOFTOKEN_QXZ7K_NESTED_RECURSIVE_MEMORY" --max-results 3
```

Step 4 — on the PR branch checkout (`30f15f4e22`), import the modified descriptor/help modules at runtime via `tsx` and print the after-patch strings to prove the new `memory/**/*.md` wording is observable from a real runtime call site (not just the diff):

```
node --import tsx _proof-c.mjs   # imports FIELD_HELP and buildPromptSection from this branch
```

**After-fix evidence**: Terminal output from the installed `openclaw 2026.5.20` runtime on the live workspace, plus runtime-imported after-patch strings from this branch (absolute paths redacted to `~`):

```
$ openclaw memory status
Memory Search (main)
Provider: local (requested: local)
Sources: memory
Extra paths: ~/.openclaw/shared-memory
Indexed: 105/105 files . 933 chunks
Workspace: ~/.openclaw/workspace-moku
By source:
  memory . 105/105 files . 933 chunks
```

```
$ openclaw memory index
[node-llama-cpp] ... falling back to using no GPU
Memory index updated (main).
```

```
$ openclaw memory search "PROOFTOKEN_QXZ7K_NESTED_RECURSIVE_MEMORY" --max-results 3
[node-llama-cpp] ... falling back to using no GPU
0.812 memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md:1-9
    Recursive memory search proof.
    Unique token for PR 85899 smoke test: PROOFTOKEN_QXZ7K_NESTED_RECURSIVE_MEMORY
    This file lives at memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md
    to verify that nested dated memory files are indexed and searchable
    by the runtime.

0.450 memory/fix-openclaw/memory-recursive-search/smoke/2026-04-27.md:1-6
0.432 memory/fix-openclaw/memory-recursive-search/pr-draft.md:1-32
```

After-patch text observed from this branch (HEAD `30f15f4e22`) by importing the modified modules at runtime:

```
$ node --import tsx _proof-c.mjs
=== schema.help.ts: FIELD_HELP['agents.defaults.memorySearch'] ===
Vector search over MEMORY.md and memory/**/*.md (per-agent overrides supported).

=== prompt-section.ts: buildPromptSection({ memory_search + memory_get }) ===
    [section header line: "## Memory Recall"]
    Before answering anything about prior work, decisions, dates, people, preferences, or todos: run memory_search on MEMORY.md + memory/**/*.md + indexed session transcripts; then use memory_get to pull only the needed lines. If low confidence after search, say you checked.
    Citations are disabled: do not mention file paths or line numbers in replies unless the user explicitly asks.

=== prompt-section.ts: buildPromptSection({ memory_search only }) ===
    [section header line: "## Memory Recall"]
    Before answering anything about prior work, decisions, dates, people, preferences, or todos: run memory_search on MEMORY.md + memory/**/*.md + indexed session transcripts and answer from the matching results. If low confidence after search, say you checked.
    Citations are disabled: do not mention file paths or line numbers in replies unless the user explicitly asks.

=== extensions/memory-core/index.ts (post-patch source lines) ===
 127      description:
 128        "Mandatory recall step: semantically search MEMORY.md + memory/**/*.md (and optional session transcripts) before answering questions about prior work, decisions, dates, people, preferences, or todos. ...",

 139      description:
 140        "Safe exact excerpt read from MEMORY.md or memory/**/*.md. Defaults to a bounded excerpt when lines are omitted, includes truncation/continuation info when more content exists, and `corpus=wiki` reads from registered compiled-wiki supplements.",

=== extensions/memory-core/src/tools.ts (post-patch source lines) ===
 240      description:
 241        "Mandatory recall step: semantically search MEMORY.md + memory/**/*.md (and optional session transcripts) before answering questions about prior work, decisions, dates, people, preferences, or todos. ...",

 410      description:
 411        "Safe exact excerpt read from MEMORY.md or memory/**/*.md. Defaults to a bounded excerpt when lines are omitted, includes truncation/continuation info when more content exists, and `corpus=wiki` reads from registered compiled-wiki supplements.",
```

**Observed result after fix**: (a) The freshly written nested file `memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md` (two levels deep under `memory/`) was indexed by `openclaw memory index` and returned as the top match (score `0.812`) for the unique token. A second nested hit from `memory/fix-openclaw/memory-recursive-search/smoke/2026-04-27.md` (three levels deep) confirms recursion is not limited to one level. (b) Importing the modified `FIELD_HELP` map and `buildPromptSection` from this branch at runtime shows the after-patch strings carry `memory/**/*.md` for both the config help text and the system-prompt memory recall guidance, matching the diff exactly. (c) The two `memory_search` / `memory_get` description strings in `extensions/memory-core/index.ts` and `extensions/memory-core/src/tools.ts` are inline string literals; the after-patch lines printed from this branch show the new wording in place. No runtime code paths are modified, only the descriptor and doc strings plus the regenerated `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256`.

**What was not tested**: This PR does not introduce or modify any runtime code path, so additional behavior tests beyond the search smoke above were not added. The recursive walk itself (`collectMemoryFilesFromDir`) is already covered by upstream tests under `packages/memory-host-sdk/`.

## Test plan
- [x] `pnpm config:docs:gen` regenerates baseline cleanly (no other drift)
- [x] Nested memory smoke search on installed `2026.5.20` returns the new nested file at score `0.812`
- [x] On-branch runtime import shows `FIELD_HELP['agents.defaults.memorySearch']`, `buildPromptSection(...)` output, and `memory_search` / `memory_get` description literals all carry `memory/**/*.md`
- [ ] CI: lint / type / config-baseline check

target.title: fix(memory-wiki): gracefully handle unparsable YAML frontmatter in vault scans (#96125)
target.body:
Related to #96125.

## What Problem This Solves

A single wiki page with malformed YAML frontmatter (e.g., a `sourceIds` list item containing inline `:` inside bold markdown — `**MEMORY.md line 235**:"..."`) crashes `wiki_apply`, `wiki_lint`, and any vault-wide scan via `compileMemoryWikiVault`. The `YAMLParseError` propagates from `parseWikiMarkdown` → `toWikiPageSummary` → `readPageSummaries` (which uses `errorMode: "stop"`) → entire vault compilation fails, even though the offending page has already been written successfully.

## Why This Change Was Made

The fix is placed in `toWikiPageSummary` — the scanning boundary — rather than `parseWikiMarkdown` — the parsing primitive:

- **Scanning callers** (`compile`, `lint`, `query`, `status`) all go through `toWikiPageSummary`. Catching here makes the entire vault resilient: one bad page produces a degraded summary with `frontmatterError` set, other pages compile normally.
- **Edit callers** (`apply`, `chatgpt-import`) call `parseWikiMarkdown` directly. They still throw on bad frontmatter so a write never silently overwrites existing metadata with empty fields.

If we caught in `parseWikiMarkdown` instead, edit paths would accept empty `{}` frontmatter → write back → **lose all metadata** (`id`, `sourceIds`, `claims`, etc.) on the page being edited. That is a data-loss bug.

The `splitWikiFrontmatterBlock` helper is extracted from `parseWikiMarkdown` so the catch block can still strip the frontmatter block from the raw content and preserve the body, even when YAML parsing fails.

## Root Cause

```text
parseWikiMarkdown()                                    markdown.ts:181
  → YAML.parse(match[1]) throws YAMLParseError         no try-catch
    → toWikiPageSummary() dies                         markdown.ts:620
      → readPageSummaries() errorMode:"stop" kills all read-with-concurrency.ts:62
        → compileMemoryWikiVault() throws              compile.ts:397-398
```

Introduced by: `1ee2733b2f` (vincentkoc, 2026-06-17) — initial memory-wiki extraction.  
The `YAML.parse` call has never had error handling since the module was introduced.  
Confidence: **clear**.

> **Note on existing PR**: PR #96177 identifies the same fix surface (catch in `toWikiPageSummary` + `frontmatterError` field + `invalid-frontmatter` lint). This PR adds a control-group regression test (valid YAML unaffected), an extra test assertion verifying the body survives degradation, and a more complete proof matrix produced with `node --import tsx` driving the real production `toWikiPageSummary`. The editorial call-path invariant (apply/chatgpt-import must still throw) is explicitly documented in the code comment.

## User Impact

- `wiki_apply`, `wiki_lint`, and any vault scan no longer fail because of an unrelated page elsewhere in the vault
- The broken page shows `invalid-frontmatter` in lint output with the exact parse error message, so users know which file to fix
- Other pages in the vault compile and lint normally

## Evidence

**Behavior addressed**: A wiki page with unparsable YAML frontmatter no longer crashes vault-wide scans. The degraded summary carries `frontmatterError` so lint reports the broken file. Valid pages are unaffected.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node v22.11.0, OpenClaw PR branch `fix/wiki-frontmatter-yaml-parse-96125` at `origin/main` = `68ead4dd80`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "
import { toWikiPageSummary } from './extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.ts';
// ... (3 scenarios: bad frontmatter, good frontmatter, no frontmatter)
"
```

**Evidence after fix** — matrix of input variants vs observed results:

| Input | frontmatterError | hasFrontmatter | id | title |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `sourceIds` with inline `:` in bold (the #96125 case) | `"Unexpected scalar at node end at line 4, column 17: …"` | `true` | `undefined` | `"Test Title"` |
| Valid `sourceIds: [source.alpha]` | `undefined` | `true` | `"synthesis.healthy"` | `"Healthy Page"` |
| No frontmatter block at all | `undefined` | `false` | `undefined` | `"Plain Page"` |

```console
$ pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  9 passed (9)

$ pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  8 passed (8)
```

Full memory-wiki CI-like shard:

```console
$ pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/
 Test Files  27 passed | 2 failed (29)
      Tests  169 passed | 4 failed (173)
```

The 4 failures are all `missing node:sqlite` (Node v22.11 < 22.19 requirement) in unrelated tests (`doctor-contract-api`, `source-sync-state`). Pre-existing infrastructure gap.

**Observed result after fix**: The bad-frontmatter page produces a degraded `WikiPageSummary` with `frontmatterError` set, the page body preserved under its original title, and frontmatter-derived fields (id, sourceIds, etc.) falling back to empty. `wiki_lint` reports `invalid-frontmatter` on the offending page. Healthy pages compile and lint exactly as before.

**What was not tested**:
- Live OpenClaw gateway with a real vault containing mixed healthy/broken pages — this would require a running gateway instance. The `toWikiPageSummary` function is exercised at unit-test level with 3 input variants covering the full matrix.
- `wiki_apply` / `wiki_lint` end-to-end via gateway — these go through `compileMemoryWikiVault` → `readPageSummaries` → `toWikiPageSummary`, which is the same call chain tested in the lint integration test.

## Regression Test Plan

```console
$ pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.test.ts extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts
```

- `toWikiPageSummary` — degrades to empty frontmatter instead of throwing (#96125)
- `toWikiPageSummary` — preserves frontmatter and body for valid YAML (#96125 control)
- `lintMemoryWikiVault` — reports unparsable frontmatter as a lint issue (#96125)

## Changes

- `extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.ts`: +25/-3 — add `frontmatterError` field to `WikiPageSummary`, extract `splitWikiFrontmatterBlock` helper, catch `YAML.parse` in `toWikiPageSummary` with degraded fallback
- `extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.ts`: +15/-0 — add `invalid-frontmatter` lint code, skip frontmatter-derived checks when `frontmatterError` is set
- `extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.test.ts`: +62/-0 — 2 new tests: degraded parse preserves body, valid YAML unaffected
- `extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts`: +41/-0 — integration test: broken page gets `invalid-frontmatter`, healthy page unaffected
